key_insight: Realistic piezoelectric-actuated wall deformations can meaningfully reduce turbulent drag, with spanwise hybrid waves delivering the strongest effect.
engineering_relevance: This work shows a practical FEM-to-DNS pathway for designing surface-actuation strategies that may improve flow control in channels and related wall-bounded systems.
method: Used finite-element analysis to model piezoelectric actuation of an aluminum sheet over practical voltage and frequency ranges.
Mapped the resulting surface deformations into DNS of turbulent half-channel flow at Reτ=200 as time-varying wall boundary conditions.
Compared upstream, downstream, and spanwise wave directions and classified deformations as travelling, hybrid, or standing waves.
evidence_signal: The abstract reports evaluated actuation ranges and DNS outcomes, with peak drag reductions of 1.6%, 5.4%, and 27.6% for upstream, downstream, and spanwise waves, respectively.
watch_item: The strongest drag reduction is demonstrated at Reτ=200 in simulations, so real-world performance may depend on structural durability, actuation bandwidth, and scaling to higher Reynolds numbers.
paper_title: Direct numerical simulations of turbulent drag reduction via piezoelectric actuation
